title: How Empaths Can Work with Their Sensitivity in Therapy
meta_description: Discover how empaths can transform sensitivity into strength during therapy. Practical guidance for feeling deeply without losing yourself.
og_title: How Empaths Can Work with Their Sensitivity in Therapy
og_description: Discover how empaths can transform sensitivity into strength during therapy. Practical guidance for feeling deeply without losing yourself.
body:
You're Not "Too Much" - You're Calibrated Differently

Let me guess. You've walked into a therapist's office - or maybe you're thinking about it - and you're already bracing yourself for the conversation where you have to explain that you feel everything. The mood of the room. The tension in someone's jaw that they haven't even acknowledged. The grief your friend is pretending isn't there. And you're terrified that your therapist is going to look at you with that polite, clinical smile and say something like, "Let's work on setting stronger boundaries."

As if you haven't tried. As if you haven't spent years trying to build walls around your nervous system, only to have them dissolve the second someone walks into the room carrying pain you can feel in your own chest.

Here's what I need you to hear: nothing is wrong with you. You're not broken. You're not "too sensitive" in some pathological way that needs to be fixed. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range of input than most people's. That's not a defect - it's a different operating system. And the right kind of therapy - the kind that actually understands empaths - can help you learn to work with it instead of against it.

Does that land?

Why Traditional Therapy Can Feel Like a Trap for Empaths

Most therapy models were built by people who don't feel what you feel. They were designed for a nervous system that operates within a narrower bandwidth. So when you sit down on that couch and your therapist asks, "How does that make you feel?" - you're not just mentally processing a memory. You're physically re-experiencing it. You're picking up on your therapist's energy, the hum of the fluorescent lights, the anxiety of the person in the waiting room, and the grief that's been sitting in your chest since Tuesday. All at once.

That's not a failure of therapy. That's a failure of fit. You don't need to be fixed - you need a approach that accounts for the fact that you're taking in more data than most people. Right?!

So let's talk about what that actually looks like in practice. Because the right therapist - or the right approach to therapy, even if you're doing it on your own - can be the difference between feeling like your sensitivity is a curse and learning to use it as a guide.

"Your sensitivity isn't a crack in your armor - it's an antenna. The goal isn't to shut it off. The goal is to learn how to tune it."

The First Thing You Need to Check: Is Your Therapist Empath-Aware?

Before we go any further, I want you to ask yourself a hard question. Is your current therapist - or the one you're considering - actually equipped to work with an empath? Because not all therapists are. And that's not a judgment on them. But if they're trained in a model that treats sensitivity as something to manage or reduce, they're going to steer you in the wrong direction.

Look for someone who understands terms like:

  • High sensitivity (the trait, not the diagnosis)
  • Energy sensitivity or intuitive sensing
  • Somatic therapy or body-based approaches
  • The difference between empathy and enmeshment

If your therapist has never heard of Elaine Aron's work on high sensitivity or hasn't considered that some people literally feel other people's emotions in their body, you're going to spend a lot of time explaining yourself instead of actually doing the work. That's exhausting. And you already carry enough.

One book that helped me understand this distinction - and that I recommend to almost every empath I work with - is The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. It's not a therapy manual, but it gives you the language to explain what's happening in your nervous system when you walk into a crowded room and feel like you're drowning. See on Amazon (paid link)

Why Grounding Comes Before Everything

Here's the thing about being an empath in therapy: you're going to feel things. That's the point. But if you haven't built a grounding practice that works for your nervous system, you're going to get flooded. And when you're flooded, you can't do the work. You're just surviving the session.

So before you even start talking about your childhood or your relationship or that thing that happened five years ago that still makes your stomach clench - you need a grounding ritual that actually works for you. Not the one your friend swears by. Not the one that sounds nice in theory. The one that drops you back into your body when the world starts to feel like it's pressing in on all sides.

For some empaths, that's physical: pressing your feet into the floor, feeling the weight of your body in the chair, touching something with texture - a piece of fabric, a stone, the arm of the couch. For others, it's breath-based: a slow exhale that's longer than the inhale, which tells your nervous system it's safe to relax. And for some, it's visual: picking a spot in the room and describing it in detail to yourself, pulling your attention out of the emotional soup and back into the physical world.

If you're not sure where to start, there's a book called The Empath's Survival Guide by Judith Orloff that walks through grounding techniques specifically designed for people who feel too much. It's practical, not fluffy. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Actually Use Therapy as an Empath (Without Getting Wrecked)

Okay, so you've got a therapist who gets it. You've got a grounding practice. Now what? How do you actually sit in a session without leaving feeling worse than when you arrived?

First, you need to learn the difference between feeling something and becoming it. This is the single most important skill for empaths in therapy. When your therapist asks you about a painful memory, you're going to feel it. That's normal. But if you don't have a way to observe that feeling without merging with it, you'll end up reliving the trauma instead of processing it.

Think of it this way: you're not the feeling. You're the one having the feeling. That distinction matters. When you can say to yourself, "I notice tightness in my chest. I notice the urge to cry. I notice that this feels familiar" - you're creating a tiny space between you and the sensation. And that space is where healing happens.

One technique that works well for empaths is to physically shift your position in the chair when a feeling comes up that threatens to overwhelm you. Move your feet. Shift your weight. Open your eyes if they were closed. That small physical reset can keep you from falling into the feeling completely.

Another approach: ask your therapist to slow down. You're not being difficult. You're being honest about your capacity. Say, "I'm feeling a lot right now. Can we pause for a second?" A good therapist will welcome that. A great one will teach you how to track that sensation without being consumed by it.

Reading the Room: When Your Therapist's Energy Is Too Much

Here's something nobody talks about: sometimes the therapist's own energy is part of the problem. You're an empath. You feel what they're feeling, even if they're not saying it. And if your therapist is anxious, distracted, or carrying their own unprocessed stuff, you're going to pick that up on top of everything else you're already working through.

That doesn't mean your therapist is bad at their job. It means you're picking up signals that most people don't. And you need to have a way to filter that out - or at least recognize it when it's happening.

If you notice yourself feeling worse after sessions, ask yourself: is this my stuff, or is this theirs? One way to check is to take a few minutes after the session to ground yourself and see what's left. If the feeling shifts or dissipates once you're out of the room, it might have been theirs. If it stays with you, it's probably yours.

There's a book called Anxiety: The Missing Stage of Grief by Claire Bidwell Smith that talks about how we carry other people's emotions without realizing it - and how to start untangling that. It's not specifically for empaths, but the approach is incredibly useful. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Resistance You'll Hit (And Why It's Actually a Good Sign)

Here's the part nobody warns you about: when you start working with your sensitivity instead of against it, you're going to hit resistance. Not from other people - from yourself. Because a part of you has been told your whole life that your sensitivity is the problem. That if you could just be less affected, less aware, less feeling - everything would be fine.

That part of you is going to fight back when you start to claim your sensitivity as a strength. It's going to whisper, "You're making this up. You're not really an empath. You're just avoiding things. You're using this as an excuse."

That voice is not the truth. It's the echo of every person who ever told you that you were too much. And the work of therapy - real therapy, the kind that actually changes things - is learning to hear that voice without obeying it.

You don't have to argue with it. You don't have to convince it. You just have to notice it, thank it for trying to protect you, and then keep going. That's it.

Practical Tools for the In-Between Moments

Therapy is one hour a week. The other 167 hours are where the real work happens. So let me give you a few things you can actually do between sessions that will make a difference.

Morning check-in: Before you check your phone, put your hand on your chest and ask yourself: "What am I feeling right now that's mine?" Just that question. It takes ten seconds. It trains your brain to separate your own emotional state from the noise you're about to absorb for the rest of the day.

The five-minute reset: When you feel yourself getting overwhelmed - at work, in a conversation, in a grocery store - find a corner, put your back against a wall, and take five slow breaths where the exhale is longer than the inhale. That's it. It's not fancy. It works because it tells your nervous system, "We're not in danger. We can downshift."

Evening release: Before bed, run your hands under cold water for thirty seconds while you consciously exhale any tension you picked up during the day. This is a somatic trick that helps your body let go of what it's been holding. It's especially useful if you've been around a lot of people or had a heavy therapy session.

If you want a deeper resource on this, there's a book called The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's dense, but it's the gold standard for understanding how trauma lives in the body - and how empaths especially need body-based approaches to healing. See on Amazon (paid link)

When Therapy Feels Like It's Making Things Worse

Let's be honest: sometimes therapy does make things worse before they get better. That's true for everyone, but it's especially true for empaths. Because you're not just talking about the hard stuff - you're feeling it in your body. And your body has been holding onto that stuff for a long time.

So if you leave a session feeling raw, shaky, or like you've been emotionally sucker-punched - that's not necessarily a sign that something is wrong. It might be a sign that you're actually doing the work. The key is whether the feeling passes. If it settles within a few hours or a day, you're probably processing. If it stays for days and makes it hard to function, you might need to adjust how you're approaching it.

Talk to your therapist about pacing. Tell them, "I need more time to ground before we go deep." Or, "Can we spend the last five minutes of session just breathing?" A good therapist will adjust. A great one will already have that built in.

The Bright Wound: Why Your Sensitivity Is Also Your Gift

I want to leave you with this. The reason I call this work "The Bright Wound" is because your sensitivity is both. It's the place where you've been hurt the most - and it's the place where your deepest knowing lives. The two are not separate. They're the same thing.

You didn't become an empath because something went wrong. You became an empath because your nervous system learned to stay open when it would have been easier to close. That takes courage. And it also takes a toll.

Therapy isn't about closing that openness. It's about learning how to carry it without collapsing under the weight. It's about learning which feelings are yours, which are someone else's, and which are just the background noise of being alive on a planet full of suffering and beauty.

You're not too much. You're not broken. You're not making this up.

You're just calibrated for a wider range. And once you learn how to work with that instead of against it, you'll stop trying to shrink yourself to fit into a world that wasn't built for you - and start building a life that actually fits.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I've put together an assessment that helps you identify where your sensitivity is showing up as a strength versus where it's still draining you. It's free, and it takes about ten minutes. You can find it at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment.
